title: At Its Inaugural WMES, IATA Calls Data Fragmentation Aviation's Biggest Maintenance Crisis — and Announces MRO SmartHub Will Go Free
summary: At the first-ever IATA World Maintenance & Engineering Symposium in Madrid this week, IATA's Director of Flight and Technical Operations identified fragmented data and disconnected systems as the core obstacle to maintenance resilience — and announced it will open MRO SmartHub to airlines at no cost.
body_markdown:
IATA held its inaugural World Maintenance & Engineering Symposium in Madrid on June 24–25, framing the industry's maintenance crisis squarely as a data and digitalization problem. The timing is right: supply chains are strained, fleets are aging, and the tools airlines actually use to manage maintenance decisions haven't kept pace with the operational complexity they're now facing every day.

## A Crisis Measured in Dollars and Grounded Jets

The numbers IATA put on the table in Madrid are hard to ignore. Stuart Fox, IATA's Director of Flight and Technical Operations, opened the symposium by laying out the full scope of what a constrained maintenance environment is costing the industry. According to his keynote, the supply chain situation cost airlines more than $11 billion in 2025 alone — with $4.2 billion of that attributable to delayed fuel savings from airlines being forced to fly older, less efficient aircraft instead of the newer ones they'd planned on operating. The aircraft order backlog has now passed 18,000 units, delivery shortfalls exceed 5,000 aircraft, and the average fleet age has climbed above 15 years.

The engine picture is particularly acute. IATA's new study with Emerton found that in March 2025, 648 Pratt & Whitney GTF-powered aircraft were grounded waiting on engine shop visits, spare engines, or parts — representing 28% of the entire GTF-powered fleet. And the forward-looking figures are sobering: annual shop visits for CFM LEAP engines alone are projected to grow from 600–800 in 2025 to over 5,000 by 2040. The MRO ecosystem isn't anywhere close to ready for that volume.

All of this matters directly to flight ops technology, because it creates pressure on the systems that sit between a maintenance event and the moment an aircraft returns to the schedule. When those systems are slow or disconnected, the pain flows upstream into flight planning, crew scheduling, and ops control.

## The Data Fragmentation Diagnosis — and a Concrete Offer

The most commercially significant part of Fox's speech was his identification of data fragmentation as the third and arguably most tractable priority IATA is addressing. The diagnosis is familiar to anyone who's worked close to airline technical operations: systems don't communicate, decisions get made with incomplete information, and time is lost moving data manually between airlines, MROs, OEMs, suppliers, and service providers.

Fox's framing was practical rather than aspirational. He described a scenario that anyone in this industry has encountered: an aircraft has a component issue, and the airline needs to know simultaneously whether the part can be repaired or deferred, whether material is available, whether the MRO has capacity, and whether the cost sits with the airline or the OEM under warranty. Right now, there's no single source for that information. A well-integrated Maintenance Information System (MIS), Fox argued, should be able to surface all of it in one place.

The concrete announcement that came with this diagnosis: IATA will make the core features of its MRO SmartHub platform available at no cost to airlines through a data participation program. The pitch is essentially a network effect play — airlines contribute data, and in return gain visibility into available used serviceable material, better tools for requesting supplier quotes, and assessments of approved alternative parts including PMA components. The data participation model is worth watching closely, because it's one of the few structures that can actually unlock the kind of cross-industry data sharing that would make a tool like this genuinely useful.

IATA also called out its recently announced agreement with CFM as a model for more open aftermarket access. That deal supports greater competition in engine maintenance by reinforcing third-party MRO access, alternative parts, and approved repairs. Fox was direct about wanting other OEMs to follow — and extending the same logic to airframe equipment and other aircraft systems.

## What This Means for Flight Ops Tech Vendors

The Operational Efficiency Track at WMES ran sessions specifically on AI for maintenance, Maintenance Information Systems, eTechLog, and digital records — which is essentially a map of where IATA sees the viable near-term investment territory for flight ops technology in the technical operations space.

For vendors in this space, the MRO SmartHub announcement is worth a close read. IATA opening a platform at no cost to airlines creates both an opportunity and a competitive question: if a standardized, industry-backed data layer starts to emerge for used serviceable material and parts visibility, what does that do to proprietary tools that have been charging for similar functionality? There's also the eTechLog angle — digital flight and maintenance log systems have been inching toward broader adoption for years, and the regulatory and operational environment is finally creating real pull.

From time spent running global deployment teams, I know that the gap between a technically sound digitalization plan and one that actually gets adopted at airline scale is mostly a data governance and integration problem, not a software capability problem. IATA's framing in Madrid suggests the industry is starting to internalize that lesson — which is why the MRO SmartHub data participation model, rather than just a feature announcement, is the interesting bet here.
questions: IATA is making MRO SmartHub's core features free to airlines in exchange for data participation. Is that model — free access in exchange for contributing data — the right mechanism to actually get airlines to share maintenance data at scale? (Yes, it can work / Skeptical, airlines will hold back sensitive data / Depends entirely on governance rules)

From your time managing global delivery and deployment across airlines at Navblue/Skywise: was data fragmentation between the MRO, airline ops, and OEM actually the biggest bottleneck you ran into, or was there something else that blocked digitalization more often?

IATA singled out the eTechLog as a key near-term digitalization priority. In your view, is eTechLog adoption at airlines still a procurement/regulatory problem, a change management problem, or something else entirely?
